title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita

Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. Fossil fuel and industry emissions are included. Land-use change emissions are not included.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between per capita fossil fuel energy consumption and per capita CO₂ emissions for various countries in 2024, with both axes plotted on a logarithmic scale. The horizontal axis measures energy consumption in kilowatt-hours, ranging from below 5,000 kWh to over 100,000 kWh, while the vertical axis measures emissions in tonnes, spanning from under 1 tonne to above 20 tonnes. Data points are color-coded by income group: lower-middle-income countries appear in pink, upper-middle-income countries in light green, and high-income countries in dark green. Circle sizes correspond to population, with India and China represented by the largest bubbles.

A strong positive correlation is visible across the dataset, forming a diagonal band from the bottom-left to the top-right. At the lower end, Pakistan and the Philippines cluster below 5,000 kWh and 1 tonne of emissions. Moving up the scale, India and Indonesia sit between roughly 5,000 and 10,000 kWh with emissions between 2 and 3 tonnes. A dense grouping of upper-middle and high-income nations, including Mexico, Turkey, the United Kingdom, Ireland, and Japan, occupies the middle range, with energy consumption between 20,000 and 40,000 kWh and emissions generally falling between 5 and 10 tonnes. At the highest levels, Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia exceed 100,000 kWh in consumption and 20 tonnes in emissions. The United States and South Korea are positioned near 60,000 to 80,000 kWh with emissions around 15 tonnes. Singapore appears as an outlier with high energy consumption near 100,000 kWh but lower emissions around 10 tonnes compared to other high-consumption nations.
